title: SEGMENT 5 — RNAi in worm: Fire and Mello, 1998

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — RNAi in worm: Fire and Mello, 1998
In 1998, two scientists named Andrew Fire and Craig Mello published a paper in Nature that, in retrospect, is one of the most important papers in modern biology.
The title was unassuming: "Potent and specific genetic interference by double-stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans.
" The result was world-changing.
They had discovered a previously unknown mechanism by which animal cells could silence specific genes, on demand, using small pieces of double-stranded RNA.
The mechanism is called RNA interference, or RNAi, and it earned Fire and Mello the Nobel Prize in 2006 — only eight years after the discovery, one of the fastest Nobel timelines in history.
There's a separate book in this series on RNAi and siRNA, and if you want the full mechanistic and therapeutic story, read that lecture.
Today, in the model-organism context, I just want to focus on one thing: the discovery happened in worm, and that's not a coincidence.
Of all the systems where one could have found RNAi, the worm was probably the only one in which the discovery was easy enough to make in a single set of experiments.
Let me explain why.
Going into the 1990s, the worm community had a puzzle.

People were trying to do gene knockdown by introducing antisense RNA — RNA whose sequence is the complement of a target mRNA — into worms, on the theory that antisense RNA would base-pair to the target mRNA and block its translation.
The technique worked, sort of, sometimes.
The results were variable.
Some labs reported strong knockdown; others reported nothing.
Fire and Mello did the controls.

The standard controls for an antisense experiment are: introduce the antisense RNA, introduce the sense RNA (which shouldn't do anything because it has the same sequence as the mRNA, just doubled), and introduce both together (a mixture).
When they ran these controls, they noticed something strange.
The mixture — sense plus antisense — gave much stronger silencing than antisense alone.
The sense RNA, which by itself did nothing, somehow potentiated the antisense effect when mixed in.
The explanation, after some more experiments, turned out to be that the active agent wasn't the single-stranded antisense RNA.
It was the double-stranded RNA formed when sense and antisense annealed together in the prep tube before injection.
When they purified pure double-stranded RNA and injected it on its own, the silencing was dramatic.

A few molecules of double-stranded RNA, injected into a worm, could silence the corresponding gene throughout the entire animal, for the entire lifetime, and the silencing was inherited by the worm's offspring.

The mechanism, worked out over the following years by many labs, turned out to involve a set of proteins — Dicer, the RISC complex, Argonaute — that recognize double-stranded RNA, chop it into ~22-nucleotide pieces called siRNAs, and use those siRNAs as guides to direct cleavage of any messenger RNA that matches the siRNA sequence.
It is, in effect, a cellular machine that takes a double-stranded RNA input and produces sequence-specific mRNA destruction.
Every animal cell, including human, has this machinery.
It evolved to fight viruses, which often produce double-stranded RNA during their replication.
Fire and Mello had discovered an entire ancient antiviral defense system that doubled as a programmable gene-silencing tool.
Why did this happen in worm rather than fly or mouse or zebrafish?
Three reasons.
One: dose-response was easy.
You could inject precise amounts of RNA into worm gonads with a fine glass needle and observe the effects in F1 progeny.
The transparency made phenotype scoring effortless.
In a mouse, an analogous experiment would have required a hundred times more labor and would not have been runnable as a screening exercise.
Two: worms ate the RNA.
This was the bonus discovery.

You could clone the gene of interest into a special E. coli strain engineered to produce double-stranded RNA, you could feed those bacteria to worms, and the worms — eating the bacteria as food — would silence the corresponding gene.
Feeding RNAi.
A trivially simple way to do genome-wide reverse genetics.

Within a few years, Julie Ahringer's lab at Cambridge had built a library covering essentially every worm gene with a feeding-RNAi construct, and anybody could do a genome-wide RNAi screen for any phenotype by feeding the right bacteria to the right worms.
Forward genetics inverted — you start with the genes, and you find the ones whose silencing produces the phenotype you care about.
In any other organism, this would have required years of construction; in worm, it was three years from RNAi discovery to whole-genome library.
Three: systemic spread.
RNAi in worms is systemic — inject it into one tissue, it spreads through the whole animal.
Inject it into the parent, it shows up in the offspring.
The worm has a set of proteins that physically transport RNAi signals between cells and between generations.
That's not true in fly, where RNAi is more cell-autonomous, and it's not true in mammals at all.
The worm's exceptional RNAi machinery made the technique vastly more powerful in worm than in any other animal.

The big idea: RNAi was discoverable in worm because of features specific to worm biology — transparency, ingestible silencing, systemic spread, fast generation time — that made the relevant experiments cheap and the readout clear.
Once discovered, the underlying mechanism turned out to be universal, present in essentially all eukaryotes, including humans.
RNA interference is now a major therapeutic modality — drugs like patisiran, approved by the FDA in 2018, use synthetic siRNAs to silence disease-causing genes in human patients.
Every RNAi drug in clinical use today traces its lineage back to two scientists studying gene silencing in worms in the mid-1990s.
This is the recurring story of model organisms.

A weird quirk of one organism's biology turns out to expose a universal mechanism that no one would have found in a more "normal" animal, because the universal mechanism's effects are too subtle in the more complex organism to detect.
The worm was the right place to find RNAi, and once found, it generalized.
That's the bet, paying off again.
Now let's see another example of the worm finding a universal mechanism — this time, the genetic control of aging.
